perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party warrants that it and its Agents have the appropriate technical and organisational measures in place against unautho rised or unlawful processing of personal data and against accidental loss or destruct ion of, or damage to, personal data held or processed by them.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges its responsibility to protect personal data.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the warranty regarding specific data protection measures, potentially violating data protection laws like POPIA in South Africa, where the contract is governed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013 (POPIA)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 19 of POPIA mandates specific security safeguards for personal data. The original clause acknowledged this obligation with a warranty, while the modified version merely expresses a responsibility without the explicit warranty regarding implementation of technical and organizational measures, making it less compliant with POPIA's detailed requirements.

perturbation.justification: The original text provided a warranty that the Receiving Party has specific measures in place for data protection. The changed text removes this warranty, only acknowledging a general 'responsibility.' This omission contradicts the intent of POPIA, as explained by the LLM, which mandates specific security safeguards. The scraped law snippet from the South African Government describes the Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013, stating it aims 'to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information.' By removing the warranty of specific measures, the modified text weakens the commitment to these minimum requirements, thus creating a legal contradiction because a legally mandated consumer protection is removed, which falls under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of each party making available to the other such Information, the parties jointly agree that any dealings between t hem shall be subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement which themselves will be subject to the parameters of the Proposal.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of each party making available to the other such Information, the parties jointly agree that any dealings between them shall be subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the explicit link to the 'parameters of the Proposal,' which could contain legally required disclosures or limitations. This could lead to the agreement being interpreted more broadly than intended and potentially violating laws related to fair competition or consumer protection implicitly addressed in the original Proposal parameters.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4 prohibits restrictive horizontal practices. If the original Proposal included stipulations to avoid anti-competitive behavior, removing the reference to it weakens the agreement's commitment to compliance, increasing the risk of violating this section.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly subjects the agreement to the 'parameters of the Proposal,' which are implied to contain legally required disclosures or limitations. The changed text removes this explicit link, potentially weakening compliance with laws addressed in the Proposal's parameters, such as those related to competition. Snippet 1 discusses 'Restrictive trade practices' and ensuring compliance. By omitting the link to the Proposal, the agreement could be interpreted more broadly, increasing the risk of violating competition laws. This aligns with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition, as the removal of a reference to legally mandated disclosures makes the agreement non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text:
8.  Adequacy of damages
8.1 Without prejudice to any other rights or remedies of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that damages w ould not be an adequate remedy for any breach by it of the provisions of this A greement and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek the remedie s of injunction, specific performance and other equitable relief for any threat ened or actual breach of any such provision by the Receiving Party or its Agents, an d no proof of special damages shall be necessary for the enforcement of the rights u nder this Agreement.
8.2 Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be cons trued as prohibiting the Disclosing Party from pursuing any other remedies available by i t, either at law or in equity, for any such threatened or actual breach of this Agreement including specific performance, recovery of damages or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text:
8. Adequacy of damages
8.2 Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be cons trued as prohibiting the Disclosing Party from pursuing any other remedies available by i t, either at law or in equity, for any such threatened or actual breach of this Agreement including specific performance, recovery of damages or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the acknowledgement that damages may not be an adequate remedy and the right to seek injunctions or specific performance. South African law, specifically the Consumer Protection Act, grants consumers specific remedies that cannot be waived. Removing the acknowledgement and right to equitable relief could be interpreted as an attempt to limit those consumer rights illegally.
perturbation.law_citation: Section 52 of the Consumer Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 52 grants courts the power to ensure fair and just consumer outcomes. By removing acknowledgement that damages may not be adequate and limiting access to equitable relief, the contract now interferes with the courts power to provide just outcomes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Third Circuit Overturns Dismissal of Whistleblower Action: Ensure That Physician-Hospital Written Agreements Are In Effect - Healthcare - United States ARTICLE 30 January 2009 Third Circuit Overturns Dismissal of Whistleblower Action: Ensure That Physician-Hospital Written Agreements Are In Effect DM Duane Morris LLP More Contributor Duane Morris LLP, a law firm with more than 900 attorneys in offices across the United States and internationally, is asked by a broad array of clients to provide innovative solutions to today's legal and business challenges. Explore Firm Details On January 21, 2009, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit overturned the dismissal of a whistleblower action filed under the False Claims Act challenging an arrangement between an anesthesiology group and a hospital. United States Food, Drugs, Healthcare, Life Sciences Philip H. Lebowitz Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. On January 21, 2009, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit overturned the dismissal of a whistleblower action filed under the False Claims Act challenging an arrangement between an anesthesiology group and a hospital. United States ex rel. Kosenske v. Carlisle HMA, Inc. and Health Management Associates, Inc. , No. 07-4616 (3d Cir.). The Court found that the arrangement did not meet the personal services exception under the Stark and anti-kickback laws. In 1992, Blue Mountain Anesthesia Associates PC, an anesthesiology group, entered into a written contract with Carlisle Hospital and Health Services ("CHHS"), giving the group the exclusive right to provide anesthesiology services at the hospital. CHHS agreed to provide office space, supplies, equipment and personnel for the group's use when providing anesthesiology services to patients. Pain management services were also referenced in the agreement; although in 1992, no pain management services were actually being provided. In 1998, CHHS built a new standalone facility containing an outpatient ambulatory surgical center and a pain clinic, located about three miles from the hospital. The anesthesiology group was given rent-free space and equipment in the pain clinic and support personnel at no charge. The group provided physicians to see patients at the pain clinic. The 1992 agreement was not amended to include this additional facility, and no new agreement was entered into between CHHS and Blue Mountain. In 2001, Carlisle HMA, Inc. purchased the hospital, surgery center and other assets from CHHS; however, CHHS did not execute a formal written assignment of its contractual rights and obligations under the 1992 agreement. Nonetheless, after the sale, Carlisle HMA and the anesthesiology group conducted their business relationship as if the agreement remained in effect. Both entities submitted claims directly to Medicare for their respective costs. Dr. Ted D. Kosenske, a former anesthesiologist with the Blue Mountain anesthesiology group, filed the False Claims Act lawsuit. He was associated with the group until 2005, when he left to open an independent pain management practice, which actively competed with Carlisle HMA's pain management clinic. Kosenske alleged that Carlisle HMA was non-compliant with the Stark and anti-kickback laws, even though when submitting its claims for facilities costs to Medicare it had certified that it was in compliance. Dr. Kosenske contended that this alleged incorrect certification made each claim false. The Stark and anti-kickback laws prohibit a healthcare provider from giving physicians any form of compensation to induce them to refer patients to the provider. Stark applies only to certain designated health services, including hospital inpatient and outpatient services. Regulations under the Stark and anti-kickback laws recognize that certain business relationships between physicians and healthcare entities are cost-effective and beneficial to patient care and
perturbation.justification: The original text acknowledges that damages might not be an adequate remedy and preserves the Disclosing Party's right to seek equitable relief (injunction, specific performance). The changed text omits this acknowledgement and the right to seek injunctions or specific performance, thereby potentially limiting the remedies available to the Disclosing Party. The Perturbation Explanation states that South African law, specifically the Consumer Protection Act, grants consumers specific remedies that cannot be waived. The Law Explanation says that Section 52 grants courts the power to ensure fair and just consumer outcomes, and that by limiting access to equitable relief, the contract now interferes with the courts power to provide just outcomes. Therefore, the omission creates a legal contradiction by potentially limiting consumer rights and interfering with the court's power to provide fair outcomes, as the change removes a right previously available.

perturbation.original_text:
3.  Records and return of Information
3.1 The Receiving Party agrees to ensure proper and s ecure storage of all Information and any copies thereof to at least the same standard as the Receiving Party keeps its own Confidential Information. The Receiving Part y shall not make any copies or reproduce in any form any Confidential Information exce pt for the purpose of disclosure as permitted in accordance with this Agreeme nt.
perturbation.changed_text:
3. Records and return of Information
3.1 The Receiving Party agrees to ensure proper storage of all Information and any copies thereof.
perturbation.explanation: This omits the requirement to store the information to the same standard as the Receiving Party's own Confidential Information and removes restrictions on copying. This could violate data protection principles under POPIA, which requires a reasonable standard of care to protect personal information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013 (POPIA)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 19(1) of POPIA requires responsible parties to secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information in their possession or under their control by taking appropriate, reasonable technical and organisational measures. Removing the clause that storage be at least to the same standard as the Receiving Party's own Confidential Information effectively lowers data protection, potentially in conflict with the 'appropriate, reasonable' requirement of POPIA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Office of Privacy and Civil Liberties | Privacy Act of 1974 Skip to main content Privacy Act of 1974 Share Facebook X LinkedIn Email The Privacy Act of 1974, as amended, 5 U.S.C. § 552a , establishes a code of fair information practices that governs the collection, maintenance, use, and dissemination of information about individuals that is maintained in systems of records by federal agencies. A system of records is a group of records under the control of an agency from which information is retrieved by the name of the individual or by some identifier assigned to the individual. The Privacy Act requires that agencies give the public notice of their systems of records by publication in the Federal Register. Click here to see a list of DOJ systems of records and their Federal Register citations . The Privacy Act prohibits the disclosure of a record about an individual from a system of records absent the written consent of the individual, unless the disclosure is pursuant to one of twelve statutory exceptions. The Act also provides individuals with a means by which to seek access to and amendment of their records, and sets forth various agency record-keeping requirements. Overview of the Privacy Act The " Overview of the Privacy Act of 1974, 2020 Edition " is a comprehensive treatise of existing Privacy Act case law. Any questions regarding the Overview may be directed to the Office of Privacy and Civil Liberties staff. Updated October 4, 2022
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 Parliament of South Africa Long title Act to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies; to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information; to provide for the establishment of an Information Regulator to exercise certain powers and to perform certain duties and functions in terms of this Act and the Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000; to provide for the issuing of codes of conduct; to provide for the rights of persons regarding unsolicited electronic communications and automated decision making; to regulate the flow of personal information across the borders of the Republic; and to provide for matters connected therewith. Citation Act No. 4 of 2013 Territorial extent Republic of South Africa Enacted by Parliament of South Africa Assented to 19 November 2013 Commenced 1 July 2020 Keywords data protection right to privacy Status: In force South African privacy legislation This article is about South African privacy legislation. For the food, see Popiah . For the 1976 TV series, see Popi (TV series) . For the Greek singer, see Popi Malliotaki . The Protection of Personal Information Act ( PoPIA or the PoPI Act ) is a piece of legislation which governs the law of data protection and privacy in South Africa . [ 1 ] The act was passed to regulate the right to privacy, as enshrined by section 14 of the Constitution of South Africa , and would work in conjunction with the Promotion of Access to Information Act . The President of South Africa assented to the Act on 19 November 2013. As part of the regulation a new government agency was created, the Information Regulator, [ 2 ] an independent body which is empowered to monitor and enforce compliance of the PoPI Act within the public and private sector. The act came into force 1 July 2020, which commenced a one-year grace period during which all South African entities were expected to become compliant. The grace period ended 30 June 2021, with the commencement of the act on the 1 July 2021. [ 3 ] [ 4 ] [ 5 ] Core Obligations [ edit ] The PoPI Act sets out several core obligations. [ 6 ] Some of the key requirements include: Personal information can only be processed: with the consent of the data subject; or if it is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract that a data subject is a party to; or it is required by law; or it protects a legitimate interest of a data subject; or if processing is necessary for pursuing the legitimate interests of the responsible party or of a third party to whom the information is supplied. Private and public entities must report data leaks to the affected people and the Information Regulator. Organisations must appoint a responsible person who must ensure compliance to the PoPI Act. Cross-border transfers of personal da
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures secure storage of information to the same standard as the Receiving Party's own Confidential Information, while the changed text removes this requirement, only stating 'ensure proper storage of all Information and any copies thereof.' The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation indicate this omission potentially conflicts with POPIA. Scraped Law Snippet 2, discussing the Protection of Personal Information Act (PoPIA), highlights that the act was passed to regulate the right to privacy, as enshrined by section 14 of the Constitution of South Africa. By removing the clause about maintaining the same security standard as the Receiving Party's own confidential information, the modified text weakens the protection of personal information, creating a legal contradiction under POPIA's data protection principles, particularly the need to secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information.
